give me feedback about this these report "Stakeholders
It is important to have a clear overview of the main stakeholders involved in the project. Described below are two key figures who play a central role in various aspects of the project. Understanding their responsibilities and involvement will be essential for successful execution and implementation of the sync tool between Microsoft Teams and Azure DevOps.

supervisor from the companyX (Internship supervisor and Innovation manager):
supervisor from the companyX, in his role as internship supervisor and Innovation Manager within companyX, represents a crucial stakeholder in the project. As an experienced innovator and supervisor, he plays a key role in the direction of the project and contributes to achieving the set goals. In his capacity as an internship supervisor, supervisor from the companyX offers continuous guidance and support throughout the entire internship process. His in-depth knowledge and experience make him not only an excellent mentor, but also a valuable source of expertise in tackling complex issues.

As Innovation Manager, supervisor from the companyX has a keen eye for automating repetitive tasks, which fits seamlessly with the objectives of the project. His involvement and insights will play a crucial role in streamlining processes and fostering innovation within companyX.
In addition to his role as supervisor, supervisor from the companyX also becomes the product owner in this project. This means that he is responsible for providing the necessary requirements and wishes for the development of the synchronization tool. Regular meetings with supervisor from the companyX will be a valuable aspect for discussing progress, receiving valuable feedback and defining key project requirements.

In short, supervisor from the companyX fulfills a versatile and crucial role in this project, where his expertise and guidance make a fundamental contribution to its successful progress.

Alicia keys (Contact for Customers and Notification Management):
Alicia Keys is my direct point of contact for matters relating to notification management. As the person who interacts with customers and is responsible for managing notifications, I trust that he can provide me with the information I need. I approach him not only when problems arise, but also when I want to gain more insight into the system, such as the frequency with which notifications are created per day. His expertise and involvement is crucial to gain a full understanding of the system and identify any optimization opportunities.

Alicia's expertise and involvement are of great value, not only as a contact person, but also as an important stakeholder in the project. He is currently busy manually completing and tracking notifications, as well as transferring them to DevOps projects when necessary. The synchronization tool will contribute significantly to optimizing his work processes, as he no longer has to manually synchronize information between Teams and DevOps. This makes his work significantly more time efficient, allowing him to pay more attention to other important tasks within notification management.



Dua Lipa (User OPAL project management system)
As a key figure in the project, Dua plays a crucial role in successfully implementing and using the synchronization tool between Microsoft Teams and Azure DevOps. With expertise in using the OPAL project management system, Dua will mainly focus on configuring and adjusting the settings of the synchronization tool.

One of her main responsibilities is to manage the settings at the project level. This includes creating and deleting the synchronization tunnels, which plays an essential role in streamlining data synchronization between the Teams and DevOps projects that belong to the OPAL projects. Dua will also focus on specifying sync details for the more advanced features the tool has to offer, such as choosing the locations notifications are synced to in DevOps.

Through her in-depth knowledge of the OPAL project management system and her involvement in fine-tuning the synchronization tool settings, Dua will be a key figure in ensuring smooth and effective execution of the project.

"

The report provides a detailed overview of the roles and responsibilities of key stakeholders in the synchronization tool project, along with the impact that their work has on project success. Overall, the structure is clear, and the presentation of each stakeholder's part is informative. Below are some points of feedback to enhance the report further:

1. Consistency with Pronouns – There's an inconsistency in referring to Alicia Keys, who is addressed with the pronouns "he" and "his," which contrast with the common understanding of Alicia Keys as a female name. Ensure that pronouns align correctly with intended gender identities, or alternatively, remove personal pronouns and use the stakeholders' role or name to avoid confusion.

2. Clarifications Needed – The report mentions a "supervisor from companyX" repeatedly, but it never states the person's name. Integrating the supervisor’s actual name, assuming it is omitted for confidentiality, could make the document appear more personalized and professional. If this is a public document, using a placeholder such as “[Supervisor’s Name]” could indicate that a specific name should be there.

3. In-Depth Information – While descriptive, the report tends to be somewhat repetitive in stating the importance of each role. For conciseness and added value, it would benefit from providing specific examples of how these stakeholders have impacted the project or any concrete actions they have taken or will take.

4. Clear Conclusion – The document lacks a concluding paragraph that brings the different descriptions together. A summary emphasizing the collaborative effort of these stakeholders and the synergy of their roles would provide a solid end to the report.

5. Proofreading – It is essential to proofread the report to correct any minor grammatical errors or awkward phrasings that may be present. Occasionally, the sentence structure is a bit cumbersome, e.g., "Alicia Keys is my direct point of contact for matters relating to notification management," could be smoothed to "Alicia Keys is the primary contact for notification management issues."

6. Specific Skills and Contributions – More concrete details on the skills or contributions of stakeholders could better illustrate their importance. For example, what specific experience does the supervisor bring that will help with innovation? Are there past achievements that can be mentioned?

7. Engagement with Other Stakeholders – Mention how these stakeholders interact with each other and the rest of the project team. Describe interdependencies or how decisions made by one may affect the others’ work.

8. Visual Elements – Adding some charts or diagrams, such as a stakeholder map or an organizational chart displaying the hierarchy and lines of communication, could visually complement the written information and make the report more engaging.

By considering these points, the report could be more concise, readable, and professional, thus providing a stronger portrayal of the stakeholders’ roles in the project.
give me the positive and area improvement

Positive Aspects:
- Detailed Descriptions: The report provides a clear and thorough description of each stakeholder's role within the project. The explanations establish a good understanding of their responsibilities and importance to the project's success.
- Role Clarity: You've articulated the dual roles of the supervisor as both an Internship Supervisor/Innovation Manager and a Product Owner, which is useful in understanding the multifaceted influence they have on the project.
- Emphasis on Impact: By explaining how the synchronization tool will benefit the stakeholders, particularly in the case of Alicia Keys, you've highlighted the practical impact of the project, which can help in stakeholder buy-in and support.
- Focus on Collaboration: The report underlines the importance of frequent meetings and discussions with stakeholders for feedback and refinement of the project requirements, signifying the collaborative nature of the project.

Areas for Improvement:
- Pronoun Consistency: As mentioned previously, ensure that pronouns consistently reflect the stakeholders' gender identities. This avoids confusion and maintains professionalism in the report.
- Proofreading and Clarity: Review the report for grammatical errors, ambiguities, or overly complex sentence structures. Simple, direct language is often more effective in business communications.
- Named Supervisor: Introduce the specific name of the "supervisor from companyX" or use an appropriate placeholder for clarity and personalization.
- Actionable Examples: Include examples of how stakeholders have contributed to the project or specific achievements to make their roles and impact more tangible.
- Conclusion Section: Include a concluding paragraph that summarizes stakeholder contributions and the overall collaborative effort, highlighting the synergy between their various roles.
- Visual Aids: Consider adding visual aids such as charts or diagrams to illustrate the stakeholders’ positions and interactions within the project framework.

By amplifying the positive aspects and addressing these areas for improvement, the report will not only acknowledge the stakeholders' contributions more effectively but also serve as a more dynamic tool for communication and project management.

Feedback on the Introduction Section of the Report:

Strengths:

1. Objective Clarity: The introduction provides a clear and precise description of the project’s motivation, its objectives, and the central goal of improving efficiency and collaboration within companyX through the integration of OPAL, Microsoft Teams, and Azure DevOps.

2. Technical Explanation: The report does a commendable job of demystifying technical jargon by explaining key terms right from the outset. This glossary reduces the risk of confusion and helps a range of stakeholders understand the project’s specific context.

3. Company Contextualization: By discussing the history, culture, and working environment of companyX, you offer a comprehensive picture that highlights the relevance of the project within the company. This also helps to understand the impact of the synchronization tool in a company with a spirited and informal culture yet a result-oriented approach.

4. Stakeholder Insights: The detailed description of stakeholders—supervisor from companyX, Alicia keys, and Dua Lipa—provides insight into everyone’s role and contributions within the project. This offers a humanizing touch to the report and underscores the collaborative nature of this initiative.

5. Process and Approach: The outlined methodologies and tools section displays a structured and methodical approach to tackling the project. By utilizing the Scrum methodology, you emphasize the iterative and adaptive strategy, attesting to the project’s dynamic nature.

Areas for Improvement:

1. Introduction Length: The introduction seems to be quite extended, possibly incorporating details that might be better suited in other sections like the company overview or somewhere in the methodology. Ensure the introduction is succinctly focused on setting up the main project objective and what the report will cover.

2. Engagement With Reader: The introduction lacks a hook or attention-grabber. It could start with a compelling statement or statistic emphasizing the importance of communication and project management to draw in readers.

3. Broader Implications: While well-explained, the introduction would benefit from framing the project in the grander scheme of companyX’s vision or digital strategy. How does this project align with broader organizational goals or technological trends?

4. Brevity in Terminology Clarification: The terminology clarification is useful, but it may be too detailed for the introduction. Consider moving the detailed explanations to an Appendix or a Glossary section and keeping only the most essential definitions in the introduction.

5. Technical Depth: The introduction could reflect more critically on the specific technical complexities of integrating OPAL, Teams, and DevOps. This would set the stage for the reader to appreciate the achievements and challenges of the project more fully.

6. Visuals: While not always necessary in an introduction, consider if a figure or schematic could assist in conveying the relationships between OPAL, Teams, and DevOps succinctly.

General Recommendations:

- Revisit the section lengths to ensure the introduction remains focused and concise.
- Possibly include an engaging fact or quote to draw the reader in immediately.
- Highlight the alignment with broader business goals or industry trends.
- Shift detailed terminology into an appendix to maintain flow.
- Reflect on the technical challenges to prepare readers for deeper insights.
- Consider adding visuals to aid in facilitating a quick grasp of the project’s fundamentals.

Feedback on the Planning Section of the Report:

Strengths:

1. Clear Phases Definition: The planning section does well to articulate the division of the 20-week internship into three distinct phases: Orientation, Development, and Completion. This structure helps set clear expectations for what each phase will encompass.

2. Preparation and Orientation: The dedicated orientation phase is commendable as it shows an understanding of the importance of immersing oneself in the company’s culture and systems before diving into development work. It indicates both thorough preparation and a methodical approach.

3. Initial Research and Proof of Concept: Undertaking initial research and creating a Proof of Concept provide evidence of proactive planning and risk management. It demonstrates an iterative approach to development, starting with validation of the core concept before expanding functionality.

4. Attention to Testing: Highlighting interim and extensive testing conveys that the project values quality assurance and is dedicated to delivering a dependable final product.

5. Acknowledgment of User Engagement: Planning for user support and smooth transition during the completion phase displays foresight and concern for the utility of the tool beyond its development. Ensuring stakeholders are comfortable with the new system is vital for successful implementation.

6. Documentation and Knowledge Transfer: The commitment to documenting the development journey and giving presentations signifies an awareness of the importance of knowledge transfer and accountability in project management.

Areas for Improvement:

1. Granular Milestones Within Phases: While the three main phases are clearly defined, providing more details about specific milestones or deliverables within these phases would enhance the reader’s understanding of the project’s progression.

2. Risk Management: More information on anticipated risks and mitigation strategies could enforce the project’s credibility. Assessing potential challenges and back-up plans is crucial for any project.

3. Stakeholder Involvement: Although user support is mentioned, the plan could include more details on how stakeholders are involved throughout the project, perhaps by including feedback loops or co-development processes.

4. Transition Between Phases: It might be beneficial to elucidate how the project transitions from one phase to the next. What criteria must be met or decisions made to move forward?

5. Allocation of Time: Providing an overview of time allocation for each phase would give a sense of pacing and the relative emphasis on different stages of the project.

6. Contingency Plans: A brief mention of contingency measures in case of unexpected delays would enhance the robustness of the plan.

General Recommendations:

- Include more detailed milestones and perhaps a Gantt chart or timeline for visual representation.
- Incorporate a risk assessment with mitigation strategies.
- Frame stakeholder involvement as an ongoing element throughout the project lifecycle.
- Clarify phase transition criteria or conditions.
- Outline the time allocation for each phase and its major tasks.
- Consider discussing contingency plans for potential setbacks.
Feedback on the System Landscape Section of the Report:

Strengths:

1. Detail-Oriented Descriptions: The detailed exposition of each system component within companyX’s landscape showcases a comprehensive understanding of how they intertwine to support work processes. This detail contributes to a fuller picture of the system environment relevant to your project.

2. Clear Functional Roles: You’ve clearly articulated the unique functionalities of each element within the system landscape — from database management and code generation to the user interface and integration platform.

3. Technical Insight: The technical architecture of the synchronization tool is well-described, offering insight into the database structure and the roles of the APIs. The focus on modularity and scalability emphasizes a forward-thinking approach to system design.

4. Integration Highlight: The explanation of how the various systems come together to support the synchronization tool offers valuable context for understanding how companyX leverages its technology stack to optimize collaboration and project management.

5. Jargon Clarification: As with the earlier section, you’ve continued to define technical terms and jargon, which helps maintain clarity for all levels of report readership.

Areas for Improvement:

1. System Interdependencies: While individual systems are well-defined, you could clarify more on how they interact with one another—especially in the context of the synchronization tool.

2. Visual Diagrams: A visual representation of the system landscape and the architecture of the synchronization tool would greatly aid in comprehension. Diagrams illustrating data flow and component interaction would offer a break from heavy textual content and can make the report more engaging.

3. User Interaction: While the technical architecture is emphasized, how users interact with the system and how this shapes the design could be further elaborated. Given the emphasis on the customer-focused nature of companyX, user experience should be a prominent aspect.

4. Impact Analysis: A deeper analysis of how these systems contribute to companyX’s bottom line, productivity, or competitive advantage would give the reader an understanding of why the project is significant beyond its technical merits.

5. Security and Compliance: Given the handling of sensitive data, the report could benefit from a discussion on how these systems address security and compliance concerns.

6. Performance Metrics: Identifying performance metrics or benchmarks used to evaluate the systems’ efficiency and the expected improvements would provide objective measures of the project’s success.

General Recommendations:

- Consider including diagrams to illustrate the system landscape and architecture, making the report visually engaging and easier to understand.
- Elaborate on how the interdependencies between systems affect project outcomes and stakeholder experiences.
- Reflect on the user interface design decisions and how they cater to the end-users’ needs and companyX’s customer-centric ethos.
- Discuss the systems in the context of their contribution to organizational goals and performance measures.
- Include a concise overview of security measures and compliance standards applicable to the systems mentioned.
- Provide metrics or expected measurable improvements introduced by the synchronization tool.

Inclusion of these aspects will not only enrich the report’s informational value but will also communicate the multifaceted impact of the synchronization tool within companyX’s system landscape.
Feedback on the Front-end, SharePoint Extension, Application Development, and Data Flow Sections of the Report:

Front-end (Vue.js):

Strengths:

1. Component-Based Approach: The description of the Vue.js application architecture using components like ‘app.vue’, ‘list.vue’, etc., clearly explains the application’s design. This structure supports modularity and reusability which is key in modern front-end development.

2. Consistency and Maintenance: The emphasis on using components such as ‘rowtemplate.vue’ for the display of individual items shows an understanding of consistency and ease of maintenance in the UI design, which will be well received by users and developers.

Areas for Improvement:

1. Use Cases: It would be useful to go deeper into specific use cases or scenarios demonstrating how users interact with the front-end components, thus giving insight into the user experience.

2. Performance Considerations: Although the structure is outlined, any considerations for the front-end performance, such as loading times or data handling, are not mentioned. This is an area that could be elaborated on.

SharePoint ListView CommandSet Extension:

Strengths:

1. Seamless Integration: The SharePoint ListView CommandSet Extension is clearly a critical piece, and its role in optimizing the user experience and enhancing notification management is well described.

Areas for Improvement:

1. Technical Detail: A bit more insight into the technical implementation and any challenges faced while integrating the extension would provide a more comprehensive understanding of this component’s importance.

Application Development and Construction:

Strengths:

1. Structured Approach: The development process, starting from the database and extending to the APIs and front-end, demonstrates a logical and methodical workflow which is commendable.

Areas for Improvement:

1. Challenges and Solutions: Including some of the challenges encountered during the development process and how they were overcome would add depth to the section.

2. Testing and Validation: The overview of the development is solid but lacks mention of how the build was tested and validated through each stage.

Data Flow in the Synchronization Tool:

Strengths:

1. Clear Role Explanation: Each component’s role in the data synchronization process is clearly explained, giving a clear picture of the tool’s functionality.

Areas for Improvement:

1. Error Handling: Details on how errors are managed and data integrity is preserved during the data flow process are missing. Including this could enhance understanding of the system’s robustness.

Realization:

Strengths:

1. Foundational Research: The description of the preliminary research and familiarization with the systems is thorough and sets a solid foundation for the development work that followed.

2. Proof of Concept: The strategic decision to focus on a Proof of Concept (PoC) before full-scale development shows a risk-averse approach aimed at ensuring the viability of the solution.

Areas for Improvement:

1. Feedback Integration: Including how feedback from the PoC was integrated into the final development could provide a narrative of iterative improvement.

2. Challenges and Setbacks: The discussion on the obstacles faced during the shift from PoC to actual implementation could offer insight into the project’s adaptability.

General Recommendations:

- Include scenarios or case studies that demonstrate user interaction with the Vue.js components.
- Discuss any potential performance constraints of the front-end and how they were addressed.
- Offer more technical details about the SharePoint ListView CommandSet Extension integration.
- When discussing application development, consider detailing the specific challenges and solutions to provide a realistic portrayal of the development process.
- Analyze error handling during data flow to add depth to the reliability of the tool.
- Reflect on how feedback from the PoC influenced the final implementation.

Broadening the report with these additional elements will increase its informative nature while also showcasing the careful consideration and adaptability that went into the project’s realization.
Feedback on the Call Converting, SharePoint Webhook, and Related Processes:

This section is packed with technical details which demonstrate a sophisticated understanding of integration between SharePoint, Azure DevOps, and Teams. Here is feedback focusing on both strengths and areas for improvement, as well as some general recommendations based on the content provided:

Strengths:

1. Step-by-Step Explained Process: The thorough explanation of the conversion process, including both initial and repetitive steps, provides a clear understanding of how the system handles data conversion between different platforms.

2. Detailed Flowcharts and Diagrams: Mentioning that there are supporting flowcharts and diagrams (e.g., Activity Diagram Call-Converter and SharePoint Webhook Reference Implementation Flow) is excellent, as these tools can help readers visually follow complex processes.

3. Handling of Edge Cases: The discussion on how default and priority mappings are handled shows a high level of thoroughness in the design, ensuring that the tool is robust and can handle a variety of scenarios.

4. Use of Webhooks: The detailed explanation of SharePoint webhooks, including their operation and management, shows a proficient use of modern web technologies to facilitate real-time synchronization between systems.

Areas for Improvement:

1. Technical Jargon: While technical accuracy is critical, the section is heavy with jargon, which may be challenging for readers who are less technically inclined. Introducing more descriptions or simplifying the language could make the content more accessible.

2. Processing of Webhook Notifications: Although the scenario of handling updated and new items is covered, there could be an elaboration on error handling and contingency planning if the webhook fails or other exceptions occur.

3. User Experience: The user’s role and experience in managing webhooks or interacting with the call conversion process are not explicitly discussed. Insight into the user’s perspective could provide a more balanced view of the system.

4. Performance Metrics: There’s an opportunity to include some performance metrics or benchmarks to showcase how the webhooks and call conversion process improve the efficiency of synchronization compared to previous methods.

General Recommendations:

1. Simplify Technical Language: Where possible, break down complex concepts into layman’s terms or introduce each technical concept with a brief explanation.

2. Visual Aids: Ensure that the flowcharts and diagrams referenced are intuitive and help in understanding the process without requiring extensive technical knowledge.

3. Discussion on User Interaction: Introduce sections that deal with how the user interacts with the system for managing webhooks and conversions, and how a good user experience is ensured.

4. Include Performance Data: Providing actual performance data can give readers an empirical grasp of the benefits of the new system.

5. Real-life Use Cases: Introduce a few real-life scenarios or use cases that demonstrate how typical synchronization tasks are handled by the new system.

6. Security Considerations: Given that the system deals with potentially sensitive project data, a brief overview of security considerations for the webhooks and synchronization process would be valuable.

By addressing these areas, the report could present a comprehensive narrative that informs readers not just about the system’s technical robustness but also its practical, everyday impact on users and the company’s operational efficiency.
Feedback on the DevOps Webhook, Advanced Functionalities, Testing Strategy, and Implementation:

Your comprehensive explanation of the technical aspects of integrating webhooks into your synchronization tool speaks to a nuanced understanding of the technology. Below I will provide feedback based on strengths, areas for improvement, and general recommendations:

DevOps Webhook:

Strengths:

1. Thorough Explanation of Operation: You have clearly explained the operational mechanism of DevOps webhooks and how they contribute to the real-time synchronization between systems, highlighting the efficient communication they enable.

2. Webhook Management: The explanation of how to manage subscriptions for DevOps webhooks, along with their creation and maintenance, is detailed and demonstrates a proper grasp of the necessary management operations.

Areas for Improvement:

1. Exception Handling: It would be beneficial to add information about how exceptions are handled when there is trouble with the webhook mechanism, or if DevOps does not send notifications as expected.

2. Impact Analysis: While the operational elements are well-explained, missing from the discussion is an analysis of the impact on workflow efficiency and how it benefits the company overall.

Advanced Functionalities:

Strengths:

1. Feature Description: Your description of the DevOps Sync Target Location functionality and the addition of SharePoint ListView CommandSet Extension demonstrates forward-thinking by providing users with additional control.

Areas for Improvement:

1. User Readability: It’s important to keep user readability in mind, ensuring that advanced functionalities can be understood and utilized easily by users of all technical levels.

Testing Strategy:

Strengths:

1. Comprehensive Test Plan: Presenting a testing strategy that goes beyond what is typically done at the company shows initiative and dedication to delivering a reliable product.

Areas for Improvement:

1. Integration with Existing Practices: A discussion of how your testing strategy integrated with or improved upon the existing practices would provide a more complete analysis of the development process.

Product to Production & Transfer:

Strengths:

1. Deployment Plan: Your description of moving the product to production and performing necessary migration and settings adjustments provides insight into the diligent approach taken in the project transition phase.

Areas for Improvement:

1. Transition Challenges: A discussion of any challenges faced during this transition phase and how they were overcome could provide valuable lessons learned.

2. User Adoption and Training: Any steps taken towards user adoption, training or change management to ensure seamless integration into daily operations could enhance this section.

General Recommendations:

- Integrate exception handling within the webhook discussion.
- Measure and discuss the impact of real-time synchronization and webhook integration on the company’s work processes.
- Ensure advanced functionality is user-friendly and accessible.
- Provide a clear linkage between your comprehensive tests and improvements in product quality and reliability.
- Discuss the deployment process, touching on how potential challenges were addressed, and how users were acclimated to the new synchronization tool.

Incorporating these insights will broaden the report’s scope and offer a well-rounded perspective on the project’s achievements and challenges, ensuring readers can appreciate both the technical mastery and the practical ramifications of the work done.

With regards to your feedback on various sections of the report, I will provide you with an overarching response that highlights both the positive aspects you've brought up and the recommended areas for improvement across the board.

The companyX synchronization tool creates a smooth interaction between the front-end, Web API and Integrations. It forms the core of the tool, with each component playing a specific role in the whole.

The starting point is in the front-end, where users are in control. They navigate through the data and edit sync mappings and project-specific values ​​via the Web API. While the front end manipulates local data, it also searches for external sound. Integrations comes into the picture here and integrates external data, such as DevOps processes, into the synchronization tool.
Conversely, Integrations receives calls from SharePoint and DevOps when notifications change there. Integrations combines this external information with the local data from the Web API, creating a new notification. Importantly, nothing is permanently saved during the synchronization process, except a status indicating which notification has been modified. This creates a transient element in the bigger picture of the sync tool.

In this way, the interplay between the front-end, Web API and Integrations unfolds as a functional exchange of data, where each component fulfills a specific role in the synchronization process.

Synchronization flow
Teams – OPAL – DevOps relationship
To successfully realize a product, it is essential to properly map the relationships between the entities involved. We have already established in previous chapters that OPAL is the internal project management system and a Teams and DevOps project can be created for projects. Previously, it was the rule that a Teams was created for each project and that they also had their own DevOps, but nowadays it is also possible that multiple OPAL projects are linked to one team. This is possible, for example, because a customer has a project in development at companyX and has purchased a permanent product such as the Diamond Online Forms.
	For our sync tool, this means that we must take into account that not all notifications from a Teams list must be sent to one DevOps project. To determine which DevOps the notifications go to, I have added an extra column in the projects table called “ApplicationName”. In the Teams list there is already a field “Application” from which you can choose from a set of standard - values, these values ​​are filled with the project name by default when generating the Teams in OPAL. The idea behind the new “ApplicationName” column is that when an incoming teams notification comes from a list linked to multiple OPAL projects, the project is searched for on the “Application” and the notification is sent to the associated DevOps.

Teams - DevOps Mapping
As described above in the operation of the sync tool, an incoming Teams notification must be added or updated in the DevOps project linked to the combination of the associated OPAL project and the chosen ApplicationName in the Teams notification. Because the objects of the two platforms are not identical and not all fields apply in the two systems, they must be converted into a suitable form. In a normal situation this can easily be done by casting the objects to each other and filling in the fields, but in the case of Teams & DevOps this works slightly differently.
Problem
A Teams notification always has the same fields, but a DevOps notification does not. This is because Azure offers the ability to assign a project to a process. The processes are used to manage DevOps notifications and are essential for optimizing workflow and project success. Azure offers out-of-the-box standard processes Basic, Agile, Scrum & CMMI. In these processes, the notification types are defined with the corresponding fields for each type (Microsoft Learn, 2023). This system ensures that the notification system can be adjusted to the needs of a project, but creates a critical problem for the synchronization tool. Because the fields and possible values ​​of a DevOps notification can differ across notification types, but also across processes, it is not possible to cast a Teams notification to a fixedly defined form.
Solution
The difference between the two platforms lies at the level of the notifications, their fields and values. To find out which value belongs in a field of a DevOps notification with a specific WorkItemType in a specific process and visa-versa, we can persist this information in the database to retrieve the information later when used. The database tables consist of four levels regarding the mapping between the two systems: DevOps Process -> DevOps WorkItemType -> FieldMappings -> ValueMappings [7.1 Database Structure]. Now that we have a way to capture the mappings, we still need to implement a functionality that makes it possible to convert a Teams notification to a DevOps notification and vice versa.
Call Converting
Flow
Converting a report from one platform to another requires a number of steps, some of which are initial and some repetitive. We start with the input. This can be a Teams notification or DevOps notification, for the explanation we will use a Teams notification as an example. For the conversion, we start with a few entities: The Teams notification, a DevOps process of the project where the DevOps notification ends up and the desired DevOps notification type. With this information, we look up the records in the database that match the data of the DevOps process and notification type. This will allow us to look up the mappings later. An activity diagram can be viewed for support and visualization in [Figure 1 - Activity Diagram Call-Converter]

Not all fields of the notification need to be synchronized to DevOps because this is platform-specific or not desired by the user (e.g.: List-related information). It is important that we determine in advance which fields will be mapped. Because Teams notifications have fixed fields, we can define this in the code.

Here we see that we want to convert the fields: title, description, priority and status.
We use the basic information to convert the notification and this works as follows: For each field we look up the field mapping in the persistent DevOps process and notification type so that we know what the field is called in DevOps (e.g.: “Status” -> “State”) and we look up the value mappings with which we can look up the field value. As shown below, there may be no field mapping for a field and a specific notification type because it is not present and we ignore the Teams field in the conversion.


 

Now that we have found the name of the DevOps field, we still have to determine which value will end up in the field. One or more value mappings are linked to the field mapping. These can be two scenarios:
There is one record whose Teams and DevOps value is '*', this means that the value of the Teams field must be transferred to DevOps
There are one or more records present, here we look for the mapping with our Teams value and put the corresponding DevOps value into our DevOpsMapping object.



By doing these steps for all Teams fields, we end up with an object that contains a list of combinations of DevOps field names and values ​​specific to the incoming DevOps process and notification type, our DevOps notification.

To go from a DevOps notification to a Teams notification, the implementation is slightly different. Because we do not know the field names of the notification, we cannot determine the convertible properties, as with a Teams notification, and cannot assume a defined object as input. To remain flexible, in addition to a DevOps process and notification type, we receive as input a list of combinations of field names and values ​​that must be mapped to a Teams notification. Here too we will try to find the corresponding field mapping and value mappings for each field.

SharePoint Webhook
Operation
The expected operation of the sync tool is mainly that the actions are automated and this does not have to be done by a companyX employee. Having an implementation for adding or updating a notification in the other system is very nice, but to initiate this process a trigger must still be present. A solution could be to periodically synchronize the notifications regardless of whether adjustments have been made, but this has the disadvantage that it may take a while before the information is synchronized. To solve this you could reduce the period between synchronizations, but that will use unnecessary resources and reduce the performance of the OPAL system.

A smart solution is to use webhooks. A webhook is, at its core, a user-defined Http callback that enables real-time communication between different web applications. They allow applications to subscribe to certain events and receive notifications when these events occur. The automated, asynchronous updates between applications eliminate the need for constant manual intervention or repetitive polling. (AppMaster, 2023)
By using webhooks that listen to new teams notifications that are added or existing ones that are changed and send our API a notification when a modification has taken place, we can initiate synchronization in a resource-efficient, performance-saving on-demand manner. SharePoint offers webhooks for various scenarios and resources, the most popular being for document libraries and lists. Customers' Teams notifications are maintained in SharePoint lists (H2.3 Terminology clarification), making this a suitable solution for our tool.
Manage subscription
Before we can receive webhook notifications, we first need to create a webhook. Here are some requirements from SharePoint and our implementation. SharePoint expects a valid resource on which the subscription should be created, a URL where the notifications should be sent and an expiration date. When requesting to create a subscription, SharePoint sends a validation request to the URL to ensure valid operation. From our tool, the requirements are synchronization-oriented. A webhook subscription should only be created when there is a Teams & DevOps associated with the specified OPAL project, because getting notifications of notifications that we cannot sync anywhere is not useful and a waste of resources. To validate the origin of the notifications, a so-called ClientState is also added. This ClientState is a random string that will be sent with every notification. From our implementation we can then check whether the ClientState matches and ignore or process the notification.
	In addition to creating a SharePoint webhook subscription, the API also supports the option to retrieve or delete a subscription. These actions can be invoked from the front-end application through the use of a simple switch allowing the user to easily manage subscriptions for the OPAL projects.

Extension of subscription
Unfortunately, the expiration date of a SharePoint webhooks subscription can be set to a maximum of 180 days. This is a choice Microsoft made, probably to remove inactive subscriptions that are forgotten by the user and save resources. We do not want an employee to have to manually create it again every 180 days from the moment a subscription was created, but for the subscription to always exist. One way to achieve this is by periodically renewing the notification. When we send an HttpRequest to create a subscription on a resource where it already exists, it is extended with the new expiration date.
	A JobService project is also present in the OPAL back-end. This job service will run the deployments periodically depending on the settings stored for individual job services in the database. In our API controller for the TeamsWebhook there is an endpoint that returns for an OPAL project whether it currently has a SharePoint webhook subscription. A written job service will check every four months for all existing projects whether there is a subscription and extend it again by 180 days. This ensures the user of constant operation of the sync tool, consistency of data between the two platforms and gives the product owner at least two months to resolve an issue if it occurs before a subscription can expire.
Processing changes
Unfortunately, when sending a notification, SharePoint does not provide the details of the modified notifications, but a list of resources to which adjustments have been made. This can allow SharePoint to bundle notifications for changed resources and notifications into one HttpRequest instead of sending multiple times with information from a single resource or notification. We will therefore first have to retrieve the adjusted notifications before we can do anything with them. SharePoint has its own system for this that works as follows: When adjusting a teams notification, adding, modifying or removing, SharePoint stores a data point in a list of changes that includes the ID of the changed item, the save the moment of change and a unique ChangeToken. From an HttpClient such as Postman or the CSOM the user can retrieve a list of changes and use the ID of the notification to retrieve them second from the teams list. A reference implementation is visualized in [Figure 2 - SharePoint Webhook Reference Implementation Flow]
	For the sync tool we only want to process new changes and not the ones we have already processed. This is where the ChangeToken comes in handy. SharePoint offers the option of retrieving changes that occurred after a certain ChangeToken, in addition to retrieving all changes. An extra column “LatestTeamsChangeToken” has been added in the projects table that keeps track of this token.
In the absence of this token (in the case of a new Teams list/SharePoint webhook subscription), the penultimate change will be retrieved and processed.



Unfortunately, the retrieved changes do not yet contain the information we need, the only useful information is the ID of the teams notification that has been modified, using the CSOM we can query the corresponding Teams list in which we are searching to teams notifications with an ID present in the list of changes.




Now that we have the Teams notifications associated with a change, we can start processing the notification. From Teams to DevOps there are three actions that can be initiated depending on the change type:
Addition
When adding a Teams notification, there will be no DevOps notification for it and we will have to create one. For the associated OPAL project, we first retrieve the process for the DevOps project and choose the -notifications type based on the Teams notification “Type”, we need this information for the notifications conversion.




After successfully adding a DevOps notification, we need to add the ID to the Teams notification so that the notifications have a reference to each other. In the notifications list there is a column called “DevOpsId” where we can enter this Id. The process of processing a new Teams call is visualized in [Figure 3 - Activity Diagram Processing Addition Teams call].

Update
Processing an updated Teams notification is quite simple, first of all we check for the presence of the “DevOpsId” field and check whether this notification is also present in the DevOps project. If this is not the case, we will empty this field in the Teams notification and cancel the synchronization.


Similar to processing a new Teams notification, we need to look up the DevOps process and notification type to convert the notification. When updating, we must take into account that the teams notification has been given a different type than the DevOps notification was initially created with, even though this will not happen often. This results in a DevOps notification from conversion that does not match the current fields in the associated DevOps notification. To resolve this, the DevOps notification type must first be adjusted before updating field values. Processing an updated Teams call is visualized in [Figure 6 - Activity Diagram Processing Update Teams call].

Deletion:
When a Teams notification is deleted, the only action that needs to be taken is to remove the reference to the Teams notification in the associated DevOps notification. This prevents a Teams notification from being found when synchronizing the DevOps notification and ensures data consistency. One problem we are encountering here is that based on the change we cannot retrieve the Teams notification from the Teams list because it has been deleted. As a result, we also do not know the number of the associated DevOps notification. One way to look up the DevOps notification is to look for a combination of the ID of the Teams notification and the unique URL of the notification in the DevOps projects linked to the Teams list. Processing a deleted Teams call is visualized in [Figure 5 - Activity Diagram Processing Deletion Teams call].






After the change is successfully processed, we need to save the DevOps notification data to the WebhookModifiedDevOpsCalls database table to ignore the new item's webhook notification and prevent the webhook loop. We will also need to update the LatestTeamsChangeToken for the OPAL projects for which notifications have been processed. By updating this token we assure the user that when calling the webhook later, only the new changes will be retrieved.



The entire process of receiving and processing a SharePoint webhook notification is visualized in [Figure 4 - Activity Diagram Teams Webhook].

DevOps Webhook
Operation
Like SharePoint [8.6.1 (SharePoint Webhook) Operation], DevOps offers support for webhooks that aim for automated and real-time communication between different systems. This solution prevents the implementation of a periodic synchronization by listening for specific events in the DevOps platform and notifying the registered systems.
A webhook is essentially a user-defined HTTP callback that subscribes to specific events. Whenever a change occurs to a DevOps work item, a notification is automatically sent to our API. This process is activated without the need for manual intervention, enabling efficient and up-to-date synchronization between Teams, OPAL and DevOps.

The use of DevOps webhooks in the sync tool brings the same benefits as the SharePoint webhooks, disabled [8.6.1 (SharePoint Webhook) Operation] and contributes to the quality and reliability of the tool. Using the DevOps webhooks is a very suitable solution for receiving and processing changed DevOps notifications.
Manage subscription
Because both SharePoint and Azure DevOps are from the Microsoft company, managing a DevOps webhook works very similar to a SharePoint webhook, but Azure offers a more diverse range of webhooks. While SharePoint can only listen to the creation and modification of Teams notifications, with DevOps it is possible to listen to development-related actions (e.g.: code pushed, pull request created, build completed, release created), but also to a notification list.
To create a webhook, DevOps expects a resource on which the subscription must be created, in our case a notification list. It also expects the specification of the entity and modification type that the subscription should listen to and a URL where the notification should be sent. Our sync tool currently only supports the ability to sync updating and deleting DevOps notifications and we choose this as the type. Unlike SharePoint webhooks, DevOps webhooks have no expiration date and the presence of a job service for renewing existing subscriptions is not applicable. Our tool requires that the project for which the webhook subscription is created has a linked Teams project, because receiving notifications that cannot be synchronized anywhere is useless.
In addition to creating a DevOps webhook subscription, the API also supports retrieving the presence or removing subscriptions. These actions can be invoked from the front-end application through the use of a switch allowing the user to easily manage the DevOps subscriptions for OPAL projects.

[Processing of changes]
Webhook-loop prevention
Problem
Webhooks are called when a change occurs to a resource that has a subscription added. In our case, this is when a notification is created, modified or deleted. The webhook sends a notification to the API and it will synchronize the notification to the other platform. Imagine a scenario where we have a Teams and DevOps with synchronization to and from the platforms. This means that when changing a Teams notification, the Teams webhook triggers and the API sends a notification. The sync tool will process the change and adjust the associated DevOps notification. The DevOps webhook then triggers and sends a notification to the API, after which our sync tool adjusts the corresponding Teams notification and triggers the Teams webhook and we end up in a loop. This loop will ensure that the same notifications are constantly updated without new information until it is interrupted by human actions or an overrun of required resources.

Solution
To prevent us from processing notifications of changed notifications by the sync tool, we must keep track of which notifications have been modified by the tool. When a notification arrives, as a result, we check whether the notification has been changed by the tool and ignore the notification. As an implementation, I added two tables: WebhookModifiedTeamsCalls & WebhookModifiedDevOpsCalls [7.1 Database Structure]. After processing a notification, a unique combination of the originating notification list & the notification number is stored here. When a notification is received, the table is searched for the presence of the notification information and, if found, it is removed from the table and the sync tool will ignore the notification. The reason why it needs to be removed is because the next time the notification is possibly updated by a user we want to synchronize this modification.

DevOps Webhook
Operation
Like SharePoint [8.6.1 (SharePoint Webhook) Operation], DevOps offers support for webhooks that aim for automated and real-time communication between different systems. This solution prevents the implementation of a periodic synchronization by listening for specific events in the DevOps platform and notifying the registered systems.
A webhook is essentially a user-defined HTTP callback that subscribes to specific events. Whenever a change occurs to a DevOps work item, a notification is automatically sent to our API. This process is activated without the need for manual intervention, enabling efficient and up-to-date synchronization between Teams, OPAL and DevOps.

The use of DevOps webhooks in the sync tool brings the same benefits as the SharePoint webhooks, disabled [8.6.1 (SharePoint Webhook) Operation] and contributes to the quality and reliability of the tool. Using the DevOps webhooks is a very suitable solution for receiving and processing changed DevOps notifications.
Manage subscription
Because both SharePoint and Azure DevOps are from the Microsoft company, managing a DevOps webhook works very similar to a SharePoint webhook, but Azure offers a more diverse range of webhooks. While SharePoint can only listen to the creation and modification of Teams notifications, with DevOps it is possible to listen to development-related actions (e.g.: code pushed, pull request created, build completed, release created), but also to a notification list.
To create a webhook, DevOps expects a resource on which the subscription must be created, in our case a notification list. It also expects the specification of the entity and modification type that the subscription should listen to and a URL where the notification should be sent. Our sync tool currently only supports the ability to sync updating and deleting DevOps notifications and we choose this as the type. Unlike SharePoint webhooks, DevOps webhooks have no expiration date and the presence of a job service for renewing existing subscriptions is not applicable. Our tool requires that the project for which the webhook subscription is created has a linked Teams project, because receiving notifications that cannot be synchronized anywhere is useless.
In addition to creating a DevOps webhook subscription, the API also supports retrieving the presence or removing subscriptions. These actions can be invoked from the front-end application through the use of a switch allowing the user to easily manage the DevOps subscriptions for OPAL projects.

Webhook-loop prevention
Problem
Webhooks are called when a change occurs to a resource that has a subscription added. In our case, this is when a notification is created, modified or deleted. The webhook sends a notification to the API and it will synchronize the notification to the other platform. Imagine a scenario where we have a Teams and DevOps with synchronization to and from the platforms. This means that when changing a Teams notification, the Teams webhook triggers and the API sends a notification. The sync tool will process the change and adjust the associated DevOps notification. The DevOps webhook then triggers and sends a notification to the API, after which our sync tool adjusts the corresponding Teams notification and triggers the Teams webhook and we end up in a loop. This loop will ensure that the same notifications are constantly updated without new information until it is interrupted by human actions or an overrun of required resources.

Solution
To prevent us from processing notifications of changed notifications by the sync tool, we must keep track of which notifications have been modified by the tool. When a notification arrives, as a result, we check whether the notification has been changed by the tool and ignore the notification. As an implementation, I added two tables: WebhookModifiedTeamsCalls & WebhookModifiedDevOpsCalls [7.1 Database Structure]. After processing a notification, a unique combination of the originating notification list & the notification number is stored here. When a notification is received, the table is searched for the presence of the notification information and, if found, it is removed from the table and the sync tool will ignore the notification. The reason why it needs to be removed is because the next time the notification is possibly updated by a user we want to synchronize this modification.

When a Teams notification is deleted, the only action that needs to be taken is to remove the reference to the Teams notification in the associated DevOps notification. This prevents a Teams notification from being found when synchronizing the DevOps notification and ensures data consistency. After the change is successfully processed, we need to save the DevOps notification data to the WebhookModifiedDevOpsCalls database table to ignore the new item's webhook notification and prevent the webhook loop. We will also need to update the LatestTeamsChangeToken for the OPAL projects for which notifications have been processed. By updating this token we assure the user that when calling the webhook later, only the new changes will be retrieved.
